title: The Bottleneck Ladder: Why Nvidia Thinks Every Supply Constraint Is a Two-Year Problem Except One

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
Jensen Huang has a surprisingly relaxed view of the supply constraints that dominate semiconductor industry discourse. CoWoS packaging, HBM memory, EUV lithography machines—he frames each of these as temporary bottlenecks that the industry "swarms" once a clear demand signal exists. His evidence: CoWoS capacity, which was the anxiety of 2023, has been doubled and doubled again to the point where "people aren't talking very much about CoWoS anymore." TSMC now scales packaging at the same rate as logic, treating it as mainstream rather than specialty technology.

The mechanism Huang describes is essentially demand-signal propagation. Nvidia convinces TSMC of future scale; TSMC's conviction then flows to ASML for EUV machines, to Micron and SK Hynix for memory. He cites a specific early meeting with Micron's leadership where he laid out predictions about AI compute demand that proved accurate, catalyzing their investment in HBM. The pattern repeats: Nvidia identifies a pinch point years in advance, invests in or partners with the relevant supply chain node, and the bottleneck dissolves within two to three years.

But Huang draws a sharp line between upstream constraints—which he considers fundamentally solvable engineering and manufacturing problems—and the downstream constraint he actually fears: energy policy. "You can't create a whole new manufacturing industry without energy," he argues, pointing to the ambition of reindustrializing the United States with chip fabs, AI data centers, EVs, and robotics. All of these compete for the same electrical grid, and unlike building another CoWoS line, energy infrastructure operates on political and regulatory timelines that no amount of demand signaling can compress.

This framing inverts the conventional wisdom about AI scaling limits. Most industry observers focus on whether TSMC can build enough leading-edge logic or whether HBM supply can keep pace. Huang essentially dismisses these as known quantities—expensive but tractable. The real question, in his view, is whether democratic energy policy can move fast enough to avoid becoming the binding constraint on an industry that wants to double annually at trillion-dollar scale.

Nvidia's proactive supply chain management extends to emerging technologies as well. Huang describes investments in silicon photonics companies like Lumentum and Coherent, partnership with TSMC on COUPE packaging technology, and even licensing patents to keep the broader ecosystem open. The goal is to shape the supply chain years before bottlenecks materialize—a kind of infrastructure venture investing that few chip designers have ever attempted at this scale.
